---
title: "Meet the Speaker"
subtitle: "Walk into a visit knowing exactly who to meet and what to say."
description: "Turn a faculty roster and a speaker itinerary into a ranked battle plan for meaningful academic conversations."
---

<svg viewBox="0 0 800 220" width="100%" style="max-height:220px;display:block;margin:0 0 1.2rem;border-radius:16px;background:linear-gradient(135deg,#0e1530,#141a30);border:1px solid #273258">
  <defs>
    <linearGradient id="g-meet-the-speaker" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#22d3ee"/>
      <stop offset="50%" stop-color="#ff4d8d"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#8b5cf6"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <filter id="soft-meet" x="-40%" y="-40%" width="180%" height="180%">
      <feGaussianBlur stdDeviation="5" result="b"/>
      <feMerge><feMergeNode in="b"/><feMergeNode in="SourceGraphic"/></feMerge>
    </filter>
  </defs>

  <!-- you, on the left -->
  <circle cx="90" cy="110" r="22" fill="url(#g-meet-the-speaker)" filter="url(#soft-meet)"/>
  <text x="90" y="160" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9aa6c7" font-size="13" font-family="system-ui">you</text>

  <!-- synapse lines from you to the speaker nodes -->
  <g stroke="url(#g-meet-the-speaker)" stroke-width="1.6" fill="none" opacity="0.55">
    <path d="M112 110 C 240 60, 360 50, 470 64"/>
    <path d="M112 110 C 250 100, 360 96, 470 130"/>
    <path d="M112 110 C 240 150, 360 168, 470 188"/>
    <path d="M112 110 C 260 110, 380 80, 500 100"/>
  </g>

  <!-- speaker / faculty nodes, ranked -->
  <g font-family="system-ui" font-size="12" fill="#e6ebff">
    <circle cx="490" cy="62" r="16" fill="#22d3ee" opacity="0.9" filter="url(#soft-meet)"/>
    <text x="490" y="66" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0b1020" font-weight="700">1</text>
    <circle cx="500" cy="128" r="14" fill="#ff4d8d" opacity="0.9" filter="url(#soft-meet)"/>
    <text x="500" y="132" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0b1020" font-weight="700">2</text>
    <circle cx="486" cy="188" r="12" fill="#8b5cf6" opacity="0.9" filter="url(#soft-meet)"/>
    <text x="486" y="192" text-anchor="middle" fill="#0b1020" font-weight="700">3</text>
  </g>

  <!-- a "match scorecard" motif on the right -->
  <g transform="translate(580,40)" opacity="0.92">
    <rect x="0" y="0" width="180" height="140" rx="12" fill="#0b1020" stroke="#273258"/>
    <text x="16" y="28" fill="#36e0d0" font-family="system-ui" font-size="13" font-weight="700">MATCH SCORECARD</text>
    <line x1="16" y1="40" x2="164" y2="40" stroke="#273258"/>
    <g font-family="system-ui" font-size="11" fill="#9aa6c7">
      <text x="16" y="62">overlap</text>
      <text x="16" y="86">recent work</text>
      <text x="16" y="110">opener</text>
    </g>
    <!-- score bars -->
    <rect x="92" y="53" width="56" height="9" rx="4" fill="#22d3ee"/>
    <rect x="92" y="77" width="40" height="9" rx="4" fill="#ff4d8d"/>
    <rect x="92" y="101" width="64" height="9" rx="4" fill="#8b5cf6"/>
  </g>

  <!-- accent dots -->
  <circle cx="300" cy="36" r="3" fill="#fbbf24"/>
  <circle cx="360" cy="200" r="3" fill="#36e0d0"/>
  <circle cx="210" cy="180" r="2.5" fill="#f472b6"/>
  <circle cx="440" cy="30" r="2.5" fill="#22d3ee"/>
</svg>

You are about to spend a week among people whose papers you cite — let's make sure you actually talk to the right ones, about the right things.

<div class="meta-row">
  <span class="pill cyan">35 min</span>
  <span class="pill violet">Intermediate</span>
  <span class="pill teal">teams of 3-5</span>
  <span class="pill amber">Research-capable AI</span>
</div>

## The goal

<div class="box goal">
<span class="box-label">The Goal</span>
Your lab has been invited to give a talk at a renowned pain-research university (say, McGill). By the end of this challenge you should be able to walk onto that campus with two things in hand: (1) a proposed <strong>talk title</strong> plus a <strong>ranked shortlist of faculty to meet</strong> — each with a one-line reason rooted in genuine research overlap — and (2) a <strong>"match scorecard"</strong> for the speakers and hosts on your itinerary, so every hallway conversation has a real opener instead of "so... what do you work on?" How you get there is up to you.
</div>

## Why it matters

The science you remember from a conference is rarely the keynote. It is the 6-minute conversation by the coffee urn where someone said "you should look at our unpublished pilot" and your next grant aim was born. But those conversations do not happen by accident — they happen when you walked in knowing that this person just pivoted from rodent models of neuropathic pain to human QST, and that your dissertation has something to say about it.

Doing this homework by hand is brutal: dozens of faculty pages, Google Scholar profiles, lab sites that were last updated in 2019. A research-capable AI can compress hours of roster-scraping into minutes — drafting overlap maps, candidate talk titles, and conversation openers. The catch, and the whole point of today: AI confidently invents affiliations, fabricates papers, and mixes up two researchers with the same surname. Your job is to move fast and then verify ruthlessly. That is exactly the muscle that keeps you out of trouble in a literature review or a grant background section too.

## Run of show

<ul class="timeline">
  <li><span class="t">0:00–0:05 · Phase 1 — Frame it (5 min)</span><br/>Instructor demos a deliberately <strong>bad prompt</strong> live and shows the mediocre output (≈2 min), reveals the GOAL (not the method), then groups do a 2-min "how would we attack this?" brainstorm. Beat that baseline.</li>
  <li><span class="t">0:05–0:20 · Phase 2 — Build it (15 min)</span><br/>Breakout groups work on their <em>assigned</em> tool. They iterate, apply at least one strategy card (role / adversarial / decompose), and log prompts + key findings in the shared doc.</li>
  <li><span class="t">0:20–0:22 · Prompt post (2 min)</span><br/>Each group pastes its <strong>single best prompt</strong> onto the shared results slide. Locked in — no more editing.</li>
  <li><span class="t">0:22–0:32 · Phase 3 — Debrief (10 min)</span><br/>2–3 groups show output; whole room compares "which AI won and why"; instructor reads the best prompts aloud and names the move that made them work. 60-sec ethics/limitations beat.</li>
  <li><span class="t">0:32–0:35 · Reset (3 min)</span><br/>Buffer: re-form groups, rotate tool assignments, advance slides. Absorbs overrun so the next match starts on time.</li>
</ul>

<p><em>Reconciliation:</em> the organizer's 5 + 15 + 10 = 30 leaves the 35-min slot 5 min short. We add a <strong>2-min prompt-post</strong> (the highest-value artifact) and a <strong>3-min reset buffer</strong> → exactly 35. Phases 1–3 keep their original lengths.</p>

## Bad prompt to better prompt

<div class="box bad">
<span class="box-label">Weak prompt</span>
<div class="prompt">Who are the pain researchers at McGill I should meet?</div>
Why it disappoints: you get a generic, possibly outdated list of famous names (and at least one who moved institutions or retired), zero connection to <em>your</em> work, no ranking, no reasons, and nothing you could actually open a conversation with. It is a Wikipedia summary wearing a lab coat — and you have no way to tell which parts are hallucinated.
</div>

<div class="box good">
<span class="box-label">Strong prompt</span>
<div class="prompt">You are a research-strategy advisor helping a pain-science PhD prepare for a lab visit. MY WORK: I study sex differences in descending modulation of chronic low-back pain, using fMRI + quantitative sensory testing in humans. TASK: Identify faculty in pain-relevant units at McGill (e.g., the Alan Edwards Centre for Research on Pain, Anesthesia, Psychology, Neurology) whose CURRENT work overlaps with mine. For each person give: (a) their main research focus in one line, (b) the specific overlap with my work, (c) a 0–100 "meet-priority" score with a one-line justification, (d) one concrete, specific opener question I could ask. Rank them. THEN, in a separate section, flag every claim you are less than 90% confident about (especially current affiliation and any specific paper), and tell me exactly what I should verify before I rely on this. Do not invent papers or titles — if you are unsure, say so.</div>
Why it works: it gives the AI an identity and your research fingerprint (so overlap is real, not generic), demands a structured + ranked output you can act on, forces self-scored confidence and an explicit verification list, and pre-empts fabrication. You leave with a battle plan and a fact-check checklist in one shot.
</div>

## Prompting moves to try

- <strong>Decompose the goal.</strong> Split it: (1) list candidate units, (2) list faculty per unit, (3) score overlap, (4) draft openers, (5) draft a talk title. Run them as separate turns so each step is checkable instead of one mega-answer you can't audit.
- <strong>Role / identity prompting.</strong> "You are a research-strategy advisor" or "you are a skeptical senior PI" changes the output more than you'd expect. Also feed it <em>your</em> identity — your methods, model system, and hot question — so "overlap" means something.
- <strong>Adversarial self-evaluation.</strong> Ask the AI to attach a 0–100 confidence score to every affiliation and paper, then to list its three least-trustworthy claims. Make it argue against its own shortlist: "Which of these matches is weakest and why?"
- <strong>Ask the AI to improve your prompt.</strong> "Here is my prompt. What's missing that would make your answer more accurate and more useful to me? Rewrite it, then answer the improved version."
- <strong>Make it cite or it doesn't count.</strong> Require a source/link per faculty claim. No retrievable source = treat as a hypothesis to verify, not a fact.
- <strong>Generate the opener, not just the bio.</strong> Push for a question only an insider would ask ("Has your group looked at whether the QST effect survives covarying for catastrophizing?") rather than "I enjoyed your work."

## Starter materials

Pick a track or do both. Everything below is paste-ready.

### Track A — The talk visit (faculty match)

Paste this scenario and roster snippet into your AI and have it rank, score, and propose a talk title. (Names below are fictional composites built for this exercise — treat the AI's "corrections" to them with extra suspicion.)

<div class="box tip">
<span class="box-label">Your lab, in one paragraph</span>
We are a translational pain lab. Core program: sex differences in descending pain modulation in chronic low-back pain, combining 3T fMRI (periaqueductal gray / rostral ventromedial medulla connectivity) with quantitative sensory testing and conditioned pain modulation. Side interest: how expectation and catastrophizing shape placebo analgesia. We're presenting next month and have ~5 slots for one-on-one meetings.
</div>

| Faculty (fictional) | Unit | Stated focus | Recent direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dr. A. Moreau | Alan Edwards Centre for Research on Pain | Spinal mechanisms of neuropathic pain, rodent | Moving into human biomarker validation |
| Dr. R. Okafor | Psychology | Pain catastrophizing & expectation | Placebo analgesia in clinical trials |
| Dr. L. Tremblay | Anesthesia | Opioid-sparing perioperative protocols | Central sensitization imaging |
| Dr. S. Nakamura | Neurology | Migraine & cortical excitability | Sex hormones × pain thresholds |
| Dr. P. Singh | Biomedical Engineering | MR methods, brainstem imaging | High-res PAG/RVM connectivity at 7T |
| Dr. C. Beaulieu | Nursing | Self-report measures, chronic LBP cohorts | Large longitudinal LBP dataset |

<div class="box goal">
<span class="box-label">Deliverable A</span>
A ranked shortlist (top 5) with a meet-priority score and reason for each, ONE proposed talk title that lands with this audience, and a flagged "verify-before-you-trust-me" list (affiliations, any paper the AI names, who actually still works there).
</div>

### Track B — Party Planner (rapid speaker recon)

You have an itinerary. Have the AI fill a scorecard so you can talk to each person like you've read their last three papers — because functionally, you have.

<div class="box tip">
<span class="box-label">Sample itinerary</span>
<strong>Tue 10:00</strong> — Keynote: Dr. Okafor, "Expectation as an Analgesic" · <strong>Tue 13:30</strong> — Lunch table host: Dr. Singh · <strong>Wed 09:00</strong> — Panel: Drs. Moreau & Tremblay (translation: bench to bedside) · <strong>Wed 16:00</strong> — Poster session walk-through with Dr. Beaulieu · <strong>Thu 11:00</strong> — Career-chat slot: Dr. Nakamura (early-career awardee).
</div>

Match scorecard template — ask the AI to return one row per person:

| Person | Their big question (1 line) | Overlap with me | Conversation opener (specific) | Avoid / sensitive | Confidence 0–100 | Must-verify |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| _e.g. Dr. Okafor_ | _Does expectation reduce pain via descending modulation?_ | _I image the PAG/RVM circuit she infers behaviorally_ | _"Have you tested whether the expectation effect tracks PAG-RVM connectivity?"_ | _Don't pitch as collaborator first contact_ | _72_ | _Confirm she gave THIS keynote; confirm placebo paper exists_ |

<div class="box goal">
<span class="box-label">Deliverable B</span>
A filled scorecard for all five itinerary people, plus your single highest-confidence opener for each — and a flag on any row scoring under ~60 that you would NOT say out loud until you've checked it.
</div>

<div class="box ethics">
<span class="box-label">Verify, then trust</span>
Research-capable AI will state a wrong current affiliation with total confidence, cite a paper that does not exist, and confuse two researchers who share a surname. Treat every name, title, and citation as a <em>lead</em>, not a fact. Before you walk into a room: confirm the person still works there and actually appears on your itinerary, and never quote a "paper" the AI named until you've found it yourself. Getting someone's work wrong to their face is worse than not knowing it.
</div>

## Debrief questions

1. Which AI's shortlist had the strongest <em>reasons</em> — and did the highest-ranked person survive a fact-check, or evaporate?
2. What did the AI get confidently, specifically wrong? (Affiliation? A fabricated paper? A merged identity?) How would you have caught it if you hadn't been looking?
3. Did role-prompting ("skeptical senior PI") or feeding your own research fingerprint change the overlap quality more?
4. Which conversation opener would you actually say out loud — and which one would embarrass you if the underlying fact were wrong?
5. How much of this could you reuse for the background section of a grant or a literature review, and what's the same verification burden?

## Level up

- <strong>Two-pass adversarial recon.</strong> Generate the shortlist with one AI, then paste it into a second and ask it to find every error, weak match, and unsupported claim. Compare what each model is willing to admit it doesn't know.
- <strong>Talk title A/B/C.</strong> Have the AI produce three talk titles tuned to three different audiences in the same building (basic neuroscientists, clinicians, methods/imaging people) and justify which slot each fits.
- <strong>Build a reusable conference kit.</strong> Turn your best prompt into a saved template with slots for [my work], [target institution], [itinerary] so you can re-run it for every meeting this year — and bolt a standing "confidence + must-verify" section onto it permanently.

---

<p style="color:#9aa6c7"><a href="../index.qmd">← Back to the Challenge menu</a> · <a href="../intro/tools.qmd">AI Toolkit →</a></p>
